name: Austin YIMBY Action
tagline: Build Austin. Housing. Transit. Infrastructure. All of it. Now.
mission:
Austin is at a once-in-a-decade inflection point where every major lever for the city's future is in play simultaneously. The city added over 200,000 residents in the last decade, making it one of the fastest-growing metros in the United States. The political decisions being made RIGHT NOW — in 2026 — will determine whether Austin becomes a city that accommodates that growth or chokes on it.

This is not a generic pro-housing organization. We exist because of a specific convergence of leverage points that makes 2026 the most consequential year in Austin's modern history:

The HOME Initiative fundamentally rewrote Austin's land use rules — but rules on paper don't build houses. We're now in the implementation phase, where individual rezoning fights, permitting bottlenecks, and enforcement decisions determine whether the reform actually produces housing. Organized pro-housing voices at every hearing is what turns policy into buildings.

Project Connect — Austin's $8.23 billion light rail system — cleared federal environmental review in January 2026, a construction contractor has been selected, and early field work has begun. But the project hangs by a legal thread right now. Attorney General Ken Paxton is challenging the entire funding mechanism at the Texas Supreme Court. Oral arguments were Feb 10. No ruling yet. If the court kills the bonds, the project collapses. This is the single largest infrastructure investment in Austin's history, and it needs active, organized defense.

Five city council seats are on the November 2026 ballot — Districts 1, 3, 5, 8, and 9. District 1 (East Austin / 78702) is an open seat for the first time in eight years. Steven Brown, a declared candidate, explicitly frames himself as anti-developer. The next council majority will determine whether Austin's pro-building trajectory continues or reverses.

445 acres of city-owned land at the Jimmy Clay and Roy Kizer golf courses have been initiated for rezoning — potentially 5,000-15,000 housing units depending on density. This is the largest single development opportunity in Austin and it will go through contentious public hearings.

And the Travis County Commissioner Precinct 4 Democratic runoff on May 26 offers extremely high leverage: turnout will be 8-12%. A small organized group can meaningfully shift the outcome.

We fight on all of these fronts simultaneously because they are all connected: transit-oriented development requires BOTH the transit AND the housing density around it. Defending Project Connect and fighting for upzoning along the rail corridor are the same fight. An organization that does one without the other is leaving half the value on the table. We are pro-building in the broadest sense — housing, transit, infrastructure — because you cannot solve any one of these problems without solving the others.
theory_of_change: All of Austin's urbanist leverage points are concentrated in 2026. The TX Supreme Court will rule on Project Connect's funding mechanism. Five council seats turn over. The golf course rezoning moves through public hearings. Federal transit funding timelines create deadlines. This concentration means that organized action in 2026 produces outsized outcomes compared to any other year. We are not a permanent institution — we are a campaign-mode operation built for a critical window. If we win the court case, elect a pro-building council, and secure the golf course rezoning, Austin locks in a trajectory toward density, transit, and affordability for decades. If we lose any of them, the window closes and the city's growth becomes a crisis instead of an opportunity.

political_positioning.description: Pragmatic, broad-tent pro-building coalition. We include progressive urbanists who see density as climate and equity policy, moderate business interests who understand that housing scarcity threatens economic growth, and libertarian-leaning property rights advocates who believe landowners should be free to build. We are not affiliated with any party. We work with pro-housing Democrats, moderate Republicans, and anyone who thinks Austin should build things. This is not an ideological project — it is a practical one.

key_policy_positions: Defend Project Connect light rail from legal and political threats
Support upzoning along transit corridors and throughout the city
Streamline permitting to reduce housing construction timelines
Ensure effective implementation of the HOME Initiative
Advocate for the Jimmy Clay/Roy Kizer golf course redevelopment as housing
Elect pro-building candidates to all 5 open council seats in November 2026
Oppose organized NIMBY efforts to block infill development
Support transit-oriented development that pairs housing density with transit access
Protect and expand Project Connect's federal funding pipeline

active_campaigns.title: Defend Project Connect
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (April 2026): Project Connect continues advancing on multiple fronts despite the pending TX Supreme Court ruling.

• January 2026: Trump administration signed off on federal environmental review (Record of Decision) — a critical milestone for ~$4 billion in FTA Capital Investment Grant funding.
• February 18, 2026: Austin Transit Partnership approved a $60 million design-build contract with Austin Rail Constructors for Phase 1.
• Early field work continues along the corridor.

Legal status: The Texas Supreme Court heard oral arguments on February 10 on AG Paxton's challenge to the funding mechanism. NO ruling yet as of April 3. The procedural question: whether the AG's office was allowed to file an early appeal. If the court sides with the city, the case returns to Travis County for trial. If the court sides with Paxton, additional appellate proceedings follow.

The project generates ~$166M/year in dedicated tax revenue and has accumulated over $400M in reserves. The $60M contract award signals ATP leadership's confidence, but the legal thread remains the single biggest risk.

This campaign coordinates rapid response preparation for the court ruling, federal funding advocacy, and public education about what Project Connect means for housing, mobility, and the city's future.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: The court ruling is binary — win or lose — but the political environment around it is not. A mobilized, visible pro-transit constituency makes it harder for state officials to celebrate a loss and easier for city leadership to find alternative paths if needed. On the federal side, demonstrated local support strengthens the FTA application. Every week of organized advocacy improves the odds on both fronts.
next_actions: Monitor TX Supreme Court docket for Paxton v. Austin Transit Partnership ruling
Prepare rapid-response communications plan for both favorable and unfavorable rulings
Coordinate with Transit Forward on joint advocacy and messaging strategy
Draft op-eds and public testimony emphasizing Project Connect's economic impact
Track federal FTA Capital Investment Grant application status and timeline
Build coalition support from business community for federal funding advocacy letters

active_campaigns.title: 2026 Council Elections
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (April 2026): The District 1 field has grown to at least 7 candidates — the most competitive open-seat race in the city. Twenty candidates total have filed across all five open council seats (Districts 1, 3, 5, 8, 9).

District 1 candidates now include: Steven Brown (Save Austin Now co-chair, anti-developer); Alexandria Anderson (MLK Neighborhood Assoc president); Simon Nahas (pro-housing advocate — supports smaller lot sizes and more building); Kyra Lorena Rogers; and at least 3 others. Nahas is the most explicitly YIMBY-aligned candidate so far. Brown's SAN connection remains a red flag. Filing period: July 20 – August 17.

Pct 4 Runoff: George Morales III vs Susanna Ledesma-Woody on May 26. LIV has endorsed Ledesma-Woody. No Republican running — winner takes the seat. Low turnout expected (8-12%).

The next council will vote on Project Connect implementation, HOME Initiative enforcement, the golf course rezoning, and a potential $687-700M bond package. This campaign develops a pro-building scorecard, candidate questionnaires, and district-level deep-dive analyses.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Council members are the final decision-makers on every zoning case, infrastructure investment, and development policy in Austin. Electing even 2-3 additional pro-building members shifts the median vote on council and locks in the current reform trajectory. The Pct 4 runoff is a bonus: in a low-turnout election, 50-100 organized voters can determine the outcome, and county commissioners control key transportation and land use decisions. Early candidate engagement locks in pro-building commitments before candidates calibrate to the general electorate.
next_actions: Develop pro-building scorecard for all current council members based on voting records
Draft and distribute housing/transit policy questionnaire to all declared candidates
Publish District 1 deep-dive analysis: demographics, development pipeline, key issues
Research Districts 3, 5, 8, and 9 candidate fields and development contexts
Research both Pct 4 runoff candidates' positions on development and transit
Prepare endorsement recommendations for Pct 4 runoff (deadline: early May)
Organize precinct-level voter outreach for Pct 4 runoff (target: 5-10 volunteers, 4 weekends)
Coordinate with League of Women Voters on candidate forum co-sponsorship

active_campaigns.title: Golf Course Rezoning — 445 Acres
active_campaigns.summary:
The City of Austin has initiated rezoning of the Jimmy Clay and Roy Kizer golf courses — 445 acres of city-owned land in southeast Austin. This is potentially 5,000-15,000 housing units depending on density — the largest single housing development opportunity in Austin's history. The site's size, public ownership, and location near existing transit routes make it uniquely suited for a mixed-use, transit-oriented development that could include thousands of housing units at multiple price points.

The rezoning will go through public hearings where organized NIMBY opposition will attempt to block or water down the proposal. We need pro-housing voices at every hearing, prepared testimony, and coalition coordination to ensure this opportunity produces the maximum amount of housing.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Public hearings are where rezoning cases are won or lost. Council members count speakers and read comments. If the only organized voices at the hearing are opponents, the rezoning gets weakened or denied. Showing up in numbers with prepared, specific testimony about the housing need and the site's potential shifts the political calculation. Coalition letters from multiple organizations amplify the signal.
next_actions: Monitor for public hearing schedule announcement from Planning Commission
Prepare template public comment supporting residential/mixed-use zoning with density
Coordinate with AURA on joint advocacy strategy and testimony logistics
Research comparable large-site rezonings in other cities for best-practice examples
Develop visual materials showing housing capacity potential at various density levels
Organize hearing attendance commitment list (target: 50+ supporters at first hearing)

active_campaigns.title: Ongoing Housing Advocacy
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (March 2026): The Pew Charitable Trusts (March 18) confirmed Austin's housing construction surge — driven by HOME Initiative and pro-building policies — produced the steepest rent decline of any large US city from 2021-2026. Austin added 120,000 units (30% increase), driving median rent from $1,546 to $1,296 (now 4% BELOW the US median). This is the proof point for everything we advocate.

Additionally, missing middle and mixed-use zoning reforms advanced through City Council on March 26, creating new zoning categories for townhomes, cottage courts, and single-stair buildings. A January study recommended 4-6 units on lots of 5,750+ sq ft. Final approval expected 2027.

Beyond the marquee campaigns, Austin's housing future is shaped by dozens of individual rezoning cases, planning commission decisions, and permitting processes every month. The HOME Initiative changed the rules, but implementation is where the rules meet reality. We monitor individual rezoning applications, provide testimony at planning commission hearings, track permitting timelines and bottlenecks, and hold city staff accountable for implementing the HOME Initiative as intended — not watering it down through bureaucratic interpretation.

This is the unglamorous, week-in-week-out work that determines whether Austin's reform moment actually translates into built housing.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Zoning reform is necessary but not sufficient. Without ongoing monitoring, bureaucratic inertia and case-by-case NIMBY opposition can effectively nullify reforms. Consistent presence at planning commission meetings and individual case hearings creates a reputation for organized pro-housing advocacy that shifts the default expectation. Staff and commissioners who know that pro-housing groups are watching and will show up are less likely to quietly undermine implementation.
next_actions: Establish weekly monitoring of Planning Commission agendas for relevant cases
Build volunteer rotation for planning commission hearing attendance
Track HOME Initiative implementation metrics: permits issued, units entitled, processing times
Document and publicize cases where HOME implementation is being undermined or delayed
Create quick-response system for mobilizing testimony on individual rezoning cases
Amplify Pew Charitable Trusts report (March 18, 2026) showing Austin's 16% rent decline as proof YIMBY policy works
Engage in missing middle/mixed-use zoning reform public comment process — support 4-6 unit allowances on 5,750+ sq ft lots
Track and publicize HOME Initiative implementation metrics: units permitted, rents, construction timelines

aligned_organizations.name: AURA
aligned_organizations.relationship: Strong ally — coordinate on most housing and land use issues. Deepest policy expertise in Austin's urbanist ecosystem.

aligned_organizations.name: Transit Forward
aligned_organizations.relationship: Primary ally on Project Connect defense and transit-oriented development. Co-coordinate rapid response for TX Supreme Court ruling.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Habitat for Humanity
aligned_organizations.relationship: Aligned on housing supply and affordability goals. Credible voice on housing need from direct service perspective.

aligned_organizations.name: Downtown Austin Alliance
aligned_organizations.relationship: Ally on urban density, downtown development, and transit. Business community perspective strengthens coalition breadth.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Chamber of Commerce
aligned_organizations.relationship: Issue-specific ally on housing supply and workforce housing. Engaged on economic competitiveness arguments for density.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Monitor
aligned_organizations.relationship: Independent local news source covering city government. Key information source for tracking council actions and development cases.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Bulldog
aligned_organizations.relationship: Investigative local journalism. Information source for government accountability and policy implementation tracking.

aligned_organizations.name: League of Women Voters
aligned_organizations.relationship: Potential co-sponsor for candidate forums in 2026 council races. Nonpartisan credibility adds legitimacy to voter education efforts.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Abundance Project
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Their family-sized housing campaign directly connects to our housing supply advocacy. More housing at every size enables family formation.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Safe & Sound
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Housing supply is upstream of homelessness — every unit of housing built reduces the pressure that pushes people out. Their public safety and homelessness accountability work addresses the downstream consequences of the housing crisis we fight upstream.

engagement_pipeline.awareness: Social media, local press coverage, op-eds on Project Connect and housing. Austin Monitor and Austin Bulldog articles as shared context.
engagement_pipeline.interest: Attend a monthly briefing on campaign updates (court ruling status, council race developments, rezoning timeline). Read our issue guides and scorecards.
engagement_pipeline.action: Show up at a public hearing, submit public comment, contact your council member, volunteer for Pct 4 runoff canvassing, attend candidate forums.
engagement_pipeline.leadership: Join the steering committee, lead a campaign working group, represent us at planning commission, coordinate rapid response for court ruling.

immediate.action: Set up candidate questionnaire for all 5 council districts
Establish TX Supreme Court ruling rapid-response protocol
Contact Transit Forward and AURA for joint rapid-response coordination

medium_term.action: Recruit 10 volunteers for Pct 4 runoff canvassing
Prepare testimony toolkit for golf course rezoning hearings
Establish planning commission monitoring rotation with volunteer commitments

strategic.action: Build multi-org coalition endorsement slate for November council elections across Districts 1, 3, 5, 8, 9
strategic.notes: Combined endorsement slate with AURA, Transit Forward, and Downtown Austin Alliance would have significant influence. Coordinate questionnaire and evaluation process.

strategic.action: Develop post-court-ruling strategy for both outcomes
strategic.timeframe: Ready before ruling (could be any week)
strategic.notes: Favorable ruling: accelerate federal funding advocacy and TOD planning. Unfavorable: mobilize for legislative fix, explore alternative funding, maintain coalition morale.

strategic.action: Connect golf course rezoning advocacy to transit-oriented development planning
strategic.notes: The golf course site's housing potential is maximized with transit access. Advocacy for both reinforces each.
